historical_cases.name: Post-9/11 U.S. Defense Buildup (2001–2003)
historical_cases.peak_move: Defense sector (ITA/LMT/RTN/GD) outperformed S&P 500 by an estimated 30–50% cumulatively over the buildup phase; individual prime contractors saw multi-year contract backlogs expand significantly
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Initial Iraq invasion phase concluded, 'major combat operations' declared over; market rotated as near-term contract award pace normalized and broader equity recovery drew capital elsewhere

historical_cases.name: Reagan Defense Buildup (1981–1985)
historical_cases.peak_move: Defense primes including General Dynamics, Lockheed, and Northrop saw sustained revenue and earnings expansion; sector re-rated meaningfully relative to industrials as Pentagon budgets rose from ~$158B to over $250B in nominal terms
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Gramm-Rudman deficit reduction legislation (1985) and subsequent budget sequestration pressure signaled a ceiling on spending growth; procurement scandals (parts pricing controversies) added negative sentiment

historical_cases.name: Post-2022 European NATO Rearmament Wave
historical_cases.approximate_duration: 约 24–36 个月（持续进行中）
historical_cases.peak_move: European defense equities (Rheinmetall, BAE Systems, Leonardo, Saab) observed 100–300% cumulative gains from early 2022 lows through 2024; U.S. primes also re-rated on NATO resupply and FMS order flows
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Theme has not fully resolved; partial cooling observed when ceasefire negotiations were discussed in late 2024/early 2025, but structural rearmament thesis remained intact as NATO spending pledges extended forward

historical_cases.name: Gulf War Defense Cycle (1990–1991)
historical_cases.peak_move: Ordnance, missile, and munitions-related names observed near-term contract acceleration; however, the cycle was relatively short as the conflict concluded rapidly and post-Cold War 'peace dividend' pressures reasserted quickly
historical_cases.exit_trigger: Swift coalition victory removed the acute demand shock; bipartisan pressure for defense budget reductions post-USSR collapse accelerated, inverting the thesis within months of conflict end

differences.description: Demand is now structurally multi-polar rather than U.S.-centric. European nations are legislating minimum GDP-percentage defense floors (2% rising toward 3%), Indo-Pacific partners (Japan, South Korea, Australia) are simultaneously expanding budgets, and Taiwan Strait tension adds a second independent demand vector. Historical buildups were typically dominated by a single sovereign buyer cycle.

The current cycle is layered with autonomous systems, drone warfare, electronic warfare, space-based ISR, and AI-enabled C2 — categories that did not exist meaningfully in prior buildups. These domains require continuous software and hardware refresh cycles rather than one-time platform procurement, potentially creating more durable recurring revenue streams for defense technology companies beyond traditional primes.

Defense industrial base capacity constraints are more acute than in prior cycles. Decades of consolidation reduced the supplier ecosystem; key bottlenecks in artillery shells, solid rocket motors, semiconductors for guidance systems, and shipbuilding yards mean that even committed budget increases face multi-year production ramp delays. Supply inelasticity may dampen near-term revenue translation but extend the duration of elevated contract award activity.

NATO's collective commitment to sustained higher spending is now treaty-reinforced and politically entrenched in a way not observed since the Cold War peak. Germany's constitutional debt-brake amendment, Poland's ~4% GDP defense target, and the EU's ReArm Europe fiscal instrument represent structural policy architecture rather than crisis-reactive supplemental appropriations — suggesting a longer baseline than typical geopolitical spike cycles.

similarities: As observed in the Reagan and post-9/11 cycles, initial contract award acceleration tends to front-run actual deliveries and earnings by 12–18 months, creating periods of valuation expansion that subsequently require fundamental earnings catch-up.

Political budget fatigue and deficit pressure have historically asserted themselves within 3–5 years of major buildup initiations, suggesting that even structurally durable cycles face periodic re-rating risk when fiscal constraints tighten.

Procurement scandals, cost overruns, and program delays have accompanied every major buildup cycle and may be observed again as capacity-constrained suppliers struggle to meet accelerated delivery schedules.
this_time_different.observation: The current defense buildup may be distinguished from most historical analogs by its simultaneous multi-sovereign demand structure and technology-layer complexity, which together suggest a cycle duration and breadth that is more reminiscent of Cold War-era sustained rearmament than the event-driven spikes of the post-Cold War period — though supply-side bottlenecks and eventual fiscal consolidation pressures remain the primary observable risk factors to monitor.

exit_signals: Formal ceasefire or peace framework in the Russia-Ukraine conflict accompanied by credible demilitarization commitments, which historically has been the most direct trigger for reversal of European rearmament spending urgency.

Announced reductions or multi-year caps in U.S. Pentagon topline budgets — particularly if accompanied by sequestration mechanisms or continuing resolutions that freeze new program starts — as observed following Gramm-Rudman (1985) and the post-Gulf War drawdown.

Meaningful backlog-to-book deterioration reported by two or more large-cap defense primes in consecutive earnings cycles, signaling that contract award momentum has peaked even if existing programs continue.

Significant multiple compression in defense sector P/E relative to broader industrials, particularly if driven by cost-overrun disclosures or program cancellations that call into question margin sustainability on fixed-price contracts.

A sustained period of NATO member nations publicly revising defense spending pledges downward — particularly Germany, Poland, or other high-profile rearmers — which would signal that the political consensus underpinning the structural demand thesis is weakening.
